sentences.text: [LAUGHS] Well, my name is Henry Wermuth, pronounced the German Vermut originally.
W-E-R-M-U-T-H.
Oh.
I see.
My passport name is actually Abram Heinriche, or Abram Heinrich, if you like.
But it became Heinz for short, and later Henry for English.
I don't think I have to spell Frankfurt for you.
Frankfurt am Main, and that's in Germany, of course.
All right.
Well, I've told you the date of birth.
Early childhood memories.
We lived in a predominantly Christian area, and had predominantly-- predominantly Christian friends.
And I did not experience a lot of anti-Semitism, and-- well, if I did at once, I-- once I sort of countered it, and it was quiet thereafter.
Of course--
--when I was getting a bit older, we moved away from that area into a more Jewish surrounding.
And when I was 10, Hitler came to power.
It wouldn't have bothered me much, but I felt a bit of disquiet amongst the adults surrounding me.
Grandparents.
My grandparents came from Poland.
And when my father was nine years old, they moved-- my father's parents-- to Germany.
He was just nine month old.
My mother's parents also came from Poland, and Gribow is the name I remember.
And my mother was about 14 when she left Poland.
And 15 when she came to Frankfurt.
Well, I do remember my-- father's parents who lived in Frankfurt.
Mother's parents lived in Vienna.
And my grandfather on my mother's side, he died two years before I was born.
Well, that's the story of my grandparents.
My father had a business when I was very young.
A shop, actually, in the main street in Frankfurt.
Later, he worked in textiles.
And later still when the Nazis ruined that trade for him, he was traveling.
He was a traveler in oil products.
That-- this was towards the very end-- maybe-- I mean, '35, '36, '37. '38, we were deported to Poland--
--but--
No.
She was a housewife.
I had one little sister, six years younger than I am.
Hannah.
Well, I remember episodes where we were quarrel-- quarrelling.
And my uncle sort of mocked me.
He asked-- he told me I'm a great heel, fighting with a six year younger sister.
And so immediate-- immediately I stop that.
But of course, now they're all-- they're all fond memories.
Yes, one could say that.
Orthodox, but my father called it modern, modern orthodox.
And that means we kept Shabbat, and yom tovim, the holidays.
Wherever he was, he said his daily prayers.
But the modern part came into it because he permitted me to go without head cover, whereas the whole school, or the rest of my classmates, did not.
Of course, in-- in school, I conformed.
But his opinion was that as there is no word in the Torah to cover one's head.
So he said, well, that's-- that's his-- his guidance.
Not unless he was praying.
You know, saying the brachot for the meal, and et cetera.
But I have got my own--
I have got my own opinion about that particular custom or usage.
When God called Moses to him, he did not ask him to put on a head cover.
He asked him to take off his shoes.
So that's my idea of why I don't usually wear a hat, although in a lot of ups and downs of my life, I ended up in a fairly religious community.
But at the same time, when I'm not actually at synagogue or not actually praying, well, I walk without a hat.
And I think this is not a very important subject.
Well, I wasn't a particularly good pupil.
I thought that to impress my classmates with football was more important than bringing home good reports.
Or rather no, that's-- that's quite wrong.
More important than impressing the teacher.

But when the time for reports came, that was the time when I really sort of regretted it all because my father was very anxious that I should bring home good reports, and well-- good for-- good in football didn't mean as much as it does today.
Yes, I did.
No.
Well, but-- not private, no.
I think so, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, eight years.
From the age of six to 14.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, that would be part of my story as-- as it will unfold, and-- well--
OK, OK.
Well, when I was 10, Hitler came to power.
I said that.
And it was time to emigrate.
We applied for a visa to America.
And at the same time, to Palestina at the time, they call it.
Palestine.
Now, the Americans had a quota system.
And so many thousands per year could emigrate there.
And in Israel-- well-- or rather Palestine at the time, it was going to be quicker.
And 1935, we sold all our belongings, furniture.
And when we were almost ready to go, a letter came from an aunt-- Aunt Golda Browner-- and she said, hang on-- stay a little longer.
The Arabs are a little bit disquiet at the moment.
Hang on, and we will call you.
Well, we went from a flat into a one-room accommodation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after, I would say, 11 month, my father decided to go back into-- again into a bigger flat because it was my-- my-- my bar mitzvah drew nearer.
And we didn't-- couldn't possibly have a reception in one room.
Oh, no.
That was still in Germany.
In 1936, I was bar mitzvah.
Well, what-- a lot to tell about that, I'm not sure.
I acquitted myself well.
I was born [HEBREW].
That means in the four days in between, where no bar mitzvah takes place.
So then there were four of us having bar mitzvah together in a very big synagogue in Frankfurt, Borneplatz.
And-- well, I acquitted myself well.
That's as much as I can tell you about my bar mitzvah.
Yes.
When we were-- when we finished school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had an uncle who had still-- still had a factory, leather goods.
And of all my schoolmates, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the only one proudly pronouncing that I had a job.
The others, of course, had to go to [HEBREW] or any other sort of training, but not a proper job.
I didn't tell the teacher that it was with my uncle.
And-- well, that would take away from the-- the glory.
Well, then after that, at the factory I did not encounter anti-Semitism either.
There were 20 workers there.
It was about 20 kilometers away from my hometown.
First, I took the train.
And later, I took my bicycle.
To commute, yeah.
But the-- the train journey cost-- was-- I earned five marks and 28 pfennig.
I had to hand in the five marks to my parents, and the 28 pfennig I could keep.
I had to borrow two more in order to go to the cinema.
But then I decided that in-- well, I guess they had-- half of that went for-- on train fares.
And I decided to go all the way by bicycle and save the train-- train fares.
And after five weeks, I could buy myself a Diana air rifle.
And it was my little six-year-old sister who hit the-- who hit bull's eye first.
And that was like in a sort of match, in a game with other boys of my age.
And my little sister, six years younger, comes along and hits bullseye first time.
And I was rather proud and--
It's a fond memory.
Anyway-- well, what should I tell you next?
Probably sometimes thoughts in-- do-- had-- in due cause of the story, thoughts come, and I have to sort of switch back.
But at the moment, I would possibly go straight on to 1938.
Yeah. '38, one morning 6-- 6-- 6 o'clock in the morning, the bells went.
The doorbell went.
And three men entered.
And I thought, gestapo.
And they ask, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Get dressed.
Come along.
And any questions were not answered.
Yeah.
So my-- my father, myself got dressed.
My mother said she didn't feel well.
She had to join us later.
So when we then later met at a station, we were one of the very few people who had still suitcases, because Mother and sister packed them suitcases.
And we were going to be deported to Poland.
Apparently Poland at that time let it be known that whoever holds a Polish passport and hasn't been to Poland for a certain amount-- number of years will lose Polish--
--citizenship, yeah.
And the Germans took this as a pretense, and gathered all those with Polish passports and put them through the border.
Over the border.
But first to the border.
Oh, yeah.
No, no, no, no, not.
It was one morning, sort of all together.
Yeah.
No, no, no, no.
There was no warning.
No.
Not at all.
Before this happened.
Well, the Nazi screw turned slowly but surely.
First I suppose-- I mean, I was young.
I didn't sort of know much about politics.
But you hear and you remember.
Jews lost their jobs, especially those in high position.
And later on, you-- there was the boycott of Jewish shops.
And-- oh.
What one could hear was before Hitler came to power, the SA, the Sturmabteilung in the brown shirts, later the-- the SS, the black shirts came later.
Marched in the town singing their national socialist songs, and many of them were very anti-Jewish.
And at a later stage, they were singing hair-raising songs like, 'when Jewish blood splashes off the knife,' et cetera, et cetera.
Sing-- you know, if 'Reds hold, Jews cry,' and things.
You know.
I mean, that's a translation of these songs.
It could get you to shivers, but nobody was killed yet.
And therefore-- if, of course, I would say if people had known what's-- what's going to happen later, they wouldn't have waited for quotas.
They would have stormed out of the country.
But everybody had-- you know, it's a difficult thing to uproot oneself.
And later-- and besides, you don't know the language, and what will you do somewhere else, and it's-- well, we knew we had to go.
But we-- everybody would have stormed out, as I say, if we had known what was going to come.
So-- well, let's go back to the deportation.
We were deported to the border.
And in a tunnels, in a subway, it was about nine hours.
Nine hours, which I at that time thought the very worst of my life.
Well, we--
No, no.
By train to the Polish border of [POLISH].
And then as-- we were so crowded in we couldn't move.
And every maybe a quarter of an hour, you could move an inch.
So therefore it was nine hours before we reached the other side.
And as we were just nearing the-- you know, going up, up, up again, and where the trains were, something happened.
And there was a sound like a beehive, I-- I call it.
People were talking.
But I was sitting already listlessly on a suitcase.
And my parents were talking to each other and couldn't agree, and had turned to me.
And they said, the Poles are closing the border.
But we can still go through, or we can go back.
What should we do?
They couldn't agree.
And I thought quickly, .
What am I leaving?
A football, a bicycle, a Diana air rifle.
I say-- I said, let's go to Poland.
And these words, of course, haunt me till today, because with these words I just-- I may have condemned them to death.
But of course, I'm told it may have happened in Germany as well.
But many relations who I have met later who were still in the queue behind us went back and later, just in time, managed to emigrate.
Anyway, we went to Poland.
We were received by the Jewish communities there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They offered us places.
They offered us tea and sympathy, and even money.
But we were going on from the first station, which was Katowice.
We're going to Krakow.
And there, soon-- I don't know how-- relations found us.
Oh, hours only in Katowice.
Just the station, actually.
Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes.
Yes.
We were offered by the Jewish community there, you-- yes, yes.
I mentioned it.
Not only tea and sympathy, but also money, even.
But anyway, in Krakow we were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] dispersed, so to say.
Mother and sister together with an uncle or other, an uncle of my father.
My grandfather's brother.
My father to grandfather's sister and her husband.
And myself in a nearby place which was the daughter of the family, also married.
Now, the people where my mother and sister went, the Kleinbergers, had two sons 10, 15 years older than myself already.
The-- where my father went, they had two sons, sort of something in between, or rather nearer my age.
And of course, the daughter had a little son of one year old.
I have to mention that because they will be part of the story later.
And where we stayed, we were eating-- except at night-- we were eating all together with Mother and sister.
The place where Mother and sister stayed, if somebody know-- knows Krakow, it was the [POLISH].
And underneath, there was the coffee-- Cafe [POLISH] where-- quite an expensive cafe with cabaret.
And now, I went to a club which was frequented by youngsters my age, and mostly, of course, emigrees.
There, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it was the only teenage year I had.
And in my book, which I am going to mention later, I wrote about some practical hoaxes I played on my-- well, on the person who wooed the same girl as I did.
And at one evening, I went back home-- or rather where we were going to eat.
And I saw a group of dancers arrive at that [POLISH].
Beautiful girl.
Beautiful girls.
But I looked at one in particular, and she looked back, and I was young and a-- a-- [LAUGHS] immediately a flame.
But it was only seconds I saw her, and then she gave a short smile and you went-- she went inside.
And I thought, why wasn't I rich and grown up, that I could follow her.
And--
Well, I suppose not everybody-- although it was a very large flat, but it was their decision, you see.
I'm-- I'm only a youngster.
I can't-- you know, decision-- decisions were made by the grownups.
It seemed sort of logical.
I-- I didn't think about that.

I mean, there-- there would have possible been enough place for the four of us, but-- well, for a length of time, I'm sure that everybody, even relations, think, well, we would be a bit crowded, and so they dispersed us as good as-- it is-- it was suiting them.
It suited us.
Anyway, I went inside, and I couldn't get the girl out of my mind.
And of course, the next day when I-- they were-- they were soon gone.
I go into more details in my book about that.
But sort of taking heed of the comparatively short time we have.
I sort of shorten my story a little bit.
Yeah?
Well, in that case I must-- now I already have shortened it so far, that I haven't told you about the hoaxes which are in the book.
I didn't-- and-- there's another episode where I sort of went down the stairs, and the same evening on the way home and I looked-- and I was half-- halfway up the stairs.
There was a-- a door which I heard giggling.
And I looked through the keyhole.
And-- and I saw that-- those dancing girls changing.
And it was in those days sort of more guarded days.
It was-- what I saw maybe would have-- people-- youngsters wouldn't have turned-- looked twice.
But it was fascinating at the time.
And I was always looking out for the girl which sort of--
--interested me, yes.
But I couldn't.
And then suddenly, I did.
As she was bending down.
But the door was between us, and I cursed it.
And at the same time, I heard it-- the door downstairs going.
You know, somebody entered the house.
And I stood up and passed by, and nonchalantly said, [SPEAKING POLISH].
Good evening.
That much, I had learned already.
And they said, good evening.
And-- but as per my youth, the image soon faded.
After all, I said to myself, she's not young anymore.
She is 24, 25 even.
Old for me.
And soon I got over this, and-- well, until, so to say, the next adventure.
